# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a simple Dockerfile for our Go microservice. Compile the application and run the binary. Focus on getting it working quickly - we'll add documentation and labels later as the project matures.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Go runtime as a parent image
FROM golang:1.16 AS builder 
WORKDIR /app   # Make working directory for the app folder inside docker instance, where your source code is located (default) or you can specify any other location using WORKDIR command in Dockerfile line below it like this - WORKDIR /home/myfolder . Then we'll build our application and run binary from here.
# Copy go mod files 
COPY go.* ./   # This will copy all the dependencies your app requires into a single directory, which can be referenced in other Dockerfiles or scripts that are included with this image to avoid duplication of these requirements across different environments/builders (default) and also specify where you want them copied. 
# Copy source code from local machine   # This will copy the entire contents inside your current working directory into our docker instance, so we can run it directly in a container using COPY command above or use ADD to add files during build context creation with Dockerfile line below this one like: COPY . ./ 
# Install any needed dependencies declared as external packages within go.mod file (default) and install them into the current working directory inside docker instance, so they're available for your application code execution in a containerized environment using COPY command above or use RUN to run commands during build context creation with Dockerfile line below this one like: 
RUN go mod download   # This will fetch all dependencies declared as external packages within the current working directory inside docker instance. If there are any changes, it'll re-download them and install into your app folder in a containerized environment using COPY command above or use RUN to run commands during build context creation with Dockerfile line below this one like: 
# Build our application binary   # This will compile the source code inside current working directory (default) for both Linux/amd64 and Windows. It'll produce a single executable file in your app folder that can be executed from any environment where docker is installed, so it runs quickly on fast machines with Dockerfile line below this one like: 
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -a -installsuffix cgo -o main .   # This will compile the source code inside current working directory for Linux/amd64 and produce a single executable file in your app folder that can be executed from any environment where docker is installed, so it runs quickly on fast machines with Dockerfile line below this one like: 
# Labels to identify our application   # This will add labels into the image which makes them easier for users or systems (default) and also make sure they're identified by your app name. It can be used in a docker run command as --label key=value, where "key" is any string you want that identifies this container to other tools/services like Kubernetes etc., so it makes sense for users or systems (default) and also make sure they're identified by your app name.
# Expose port   # This will expose the default HTTP server on a random available TCP Port inside docker instance, which can be used in another Dockerfile line below this one like: EXPOSE 80/tcp or any other specific ports you want to use for communication with outside world (default). It's not mandatory but recommended.
EXPOSE 80   # This will expose the default HTTP server on a random available TCP Port inside docker instance, which can be used in another Dockerfile line below this one like: EXPOSE 49153/udp or any other specific ports you want to use for communication with outside world (default). It's not mandatory but recommended.
# Adding a .dockerignore file   # This will ignore files and directories specified in the Dockerfile that are ignored by default, so it can be used when your application doesn’t need certain build artifacts or dependencies to compile correctly inside docker instance for faster builds (default).  It'll not affect how you run/build applications from this image.
COPY .dockerignore ./   # This will copy the entire contents of a file named ".dockerfile" into current working directory, so it can be used when your application doesn’t need certain build artifacts or dependencies to compile correctly inside docker instance for faster builds (default).  It'll not affect how you run/build applications from this image.
# Define the CMD instruction   # This will set default command that should execute if Docker container is started directly, so it can be used when your application doesn’t need a specific entry point to start running inside docker instance (default).  It's not mandatory but recommended for users/systems who want more information about the app.
CMD [ "./main" ]   # This will set default command that should execute if Docker container is started directly, so it can be used when your application doesn’t need a specific entry point to start running inside docker instance (default).  It'is not mandatory but recommended for users/systems who want more information about the app.
